doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectatic changes in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and basal segments on the left. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified.
No pleural effusion.
Trachea and central bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.
No coronary calcification.
Pericardial effusion measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Transpedicular fixation at T12-L2. Decreased vertebral body height at T5-T6 up to 30%. Osteosclerotic lesion in the head of the left humerus measuring up to 5 mm. Healing fracture of the posterior left 10th rib.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.

Thyroid gland: In the right thyroid lobe, there is a hypodense lesion with well-defined margins and fluid density, measuring up to 7 × 6 mm.
doc_impression:
1. No pulmonary nodules requiring follow-up.

2. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

3. Small pericardial effusion.

4. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body height at T5-T6. Osteosclerotic lesion in the head of the left humerus (Bone-RADS 1), likely enostosis. Transpedicular fixation at T12-L2. Healing fracture of the left 10th rib.

5. Thyroid nodular changes: per ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150), no further follow-up is required.
val_findings:

Trachea and central bronchi are patent. Mild wall thickening and a small amount of secretions in the subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No coronary calcification. Pericardial effusion measures up to 8 mm in thickness.

val_impression:
1. No pulmonary nodules requiring follow-up. Mild wall thickening and small amount of secretion of the subsegmental bronchi may represent bronchitis.

2. Trace pericardial effusion.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Acute pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (valley fever). 2/26. Fluconazole. Productive cough. Abnormal blood work.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 17.06 mGy; total DLP 596.46 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid subpleural nodule measures 9 mm (volume 91.0 mm³) in the left lower lobe (series 4, image 173). Linear atelectatic changes in the basal segments of both lungs, clinically insignificant. Calcification in the apical right lung, clinically insignificant. No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical pleural thickening bilaterally. Mild thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi, with mild cylindrical dilatation of the lumina in lung bases bilaterally.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: Enlarged 4L lymph node measures up to 13 mm in short axis. Small hiatal hernia with partial herniation of the gastric cardia into the posterior inferior mediastinum.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic valve calcification. Cardiac pacemaker is present.

Great vessels: Ascending thoracic aorta is dilated to 4.7 cm, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.0 cm, right pulmonary artery to 2.7 cm, and left pulmonary artery to 2.5 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 73 HU, T12 - 56 HU, L1 - 63 HU. Decreased height of the T12 vertebral body by 21%.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Solid 9 mm nodule in the left lower lobe. According to the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, in both low-risk and high-risk patients, chest CT in 3 months should be considered. PET/CT may be performed for further characterization of the nodule.

2. Cylindrical bronchiectasis.

3. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

4. Calcification of the thoracic aorta.

5. Aortic valve calcification.

6. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

7. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

8. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

9. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

10. Hiatal hernia.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid nodule in the left lower lobe measures 9 mm (series 4, image 173). Calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe measures 4 mm, requires no follow-up. Linear atelectasis in bilateral lung bases and right middle lobe. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic valve calcification. Cardiac pacemaker.

Great vessels: Ascending thoracic aorta is dilated up to 4.7 cm. Aortic wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.0 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae, most pronounced in the T12 vertebral body, measuring up to +56 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the shoulder joints bilaterally.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the left lower lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with high or low risk of lung cancer, follow-up chest CT at 3 months. If stable, additional follow-up chest CT at 18 to 24 months. Alternatively (or additionally) PET/CT or tissue sampling could be performed.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe.
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

3. Cardiac pacemaker.

4. Calcification of the thoracic aorta. Dilatation of the ascending aorta. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. CT signs of bone demineralization.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Solitary lung nodule.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 15.41 mGy; total DLP 590.2 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Linear atelectatic bands in the left basal segments.
Mixed emphysema in the lungs, with air bullae measuring up to 1.5 cm.
In the right upper lobe, there is a cavitary lesion with overall dimensions of about 2.7 × 2.1 cm (series 5, image 279), with a solid component measuring about 1.9 × 1.0 cm and a linear hyperdense inclusion up to 4 mm. The contours of the above-described lesion are irregular, with pleural tags to the costal pleura and, less prominently, to the mediastinal pleura. Adjacent subsegmental bronchi of segment 3 are retracted toward the lesion, and their walls are thickened.
A few perifissural micronodules bilaterally.
Otherwise: No nodules requiring CT follow-up are identified in the lungs.
No pleural effusion.
Trachea and central bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:
Cardiac chambers are enlarged.
Aortic valve calcification.
Fluid in the pericardial cavity measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness.
Mild coronary artery calcification in the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels:
Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcification of the wall is present.
Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased bone density in the L1 vertebral body to +149 HU. Schmorl's node of the superior endplate of L2 measuring up to 1.1 cm.

Upper abdomen:
A few hypodense lesions in the liver parenchyma, within the imaged field, with well-defined smooth contours and fluid density, measuring up to 1.1 × 1.0 cm.
Lymph node in the lesser omentum is enlarged to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 4, image 253), and along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.1 cm in short axis (image 249).

Thyroid gland:
Asymmetric, left greater than right. In the left lobe, there is a heterogeneous nodular lesion measuring up to 3.4 × 2.4 cm with hypodense areas.
doc_impression:
1. CT findings of a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe, which, in correlation with clinical, laboratory, and historical data, may represent an aspergilloma. No other nodules requiring CT follow-up are identified in the lungs.
Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer.
Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Mild coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Small pericardial effusion.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased density of the L1 vertebral body. Schmorl's node at L2.

8. Hypodense liver lesions: in low-risk patients (correlate with clinical history), according to "Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee," 2017, CT follow-up is not required.

9. Abdominal lymphadenopathy.

10. Nodular changes of the thyroid gland: according to "Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee," 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Diffuse multiple pulmonary cysts with thin walls and upper lobe predominance. In the right upper lobe, there is a cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body. Overall dimensions are about 2.7 × 2.1 cm (series 5, image 279). A solid component measures 1.9 × 1.0 cm, and a foreign body measures up to 4 mm (3000 HU). There are multiple strands to the costal pleura and to the mediastinal pleura. Adjacent subsegmental bronchi are retracted toward the lesion, and their walls are thickened, with contents in their lumen. Two calcified nodules measure up to 4 mm in both lungs (image 127 on the right and image 78 on the left), healed granulomas, require no follow-up. Mild interstitial changes in lung bases (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Trachea and central bronchi are patent. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. Aortic valve calcification. Fluid in the pericardial cavity measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated (3.9 cm), with atherosclerotic plaques. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament.

Upper abdomen: Multiple hypodense lesions in the liver parenchyma with well-defined smooth contours and fluid density, measuring up to 9 mm. Lymph node in the lesser omentum is enlarged to 1.1 cm in short axis (series 4, image 253), and along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.1 cm in short axis (image 249).

Thyroid gland: Asymmetric, left lobe is enlarged, heterogeneous with nodular lesions.
val_impression:
1. The right upper lobe cavity with bronchial connection, irregular wall, solid mural component, and metal foreign body may represent a pulmonary mycetoma (aspergilloma) and requires follow-up.

2. Diffuse cystic lung disease with upper lobe predominance. According to Managing Incidental Findings on Thoracic CT: Lung Findings. A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2021, consider pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis.

3. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi may represent bronchitis.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Small-volume pericardial effusion.

7. Hypodense liver lesions. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

8. Abdominal lymphadenopathy.

9. Heterogeneous and enlarged left thyroid lobe. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid nodule with irregular margins measuring 1.5 × 1.0 cm in the right upper lobe (series 6, image 60), unchanged.
Linear atelectatic changes in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and left basal segments.
Multiple peripheral micronodules adjacent to this nodule, unchanged.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.
No significant pericardial effusion.
No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression: 1. Solid nodule in the right upper lobe measuring 1.5 × 1.0 cm with peripheral micronodules, stable compared with October 07, 2025. Given the history of suspected coccidioidomycosis on studies dated October 07, 2025 and December 22, 2023, and the presence of cavitary consolidation in the same region on the earlier study dated December 22, 2023, this lesion likely represents a postinfectious scar change (granuloma). In the absence of clinical symptoms, further CT follow-up is not required; alternatively, consider repeat CT in 12-24 months.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid nodule measuring 1.5 × 1.0 cm in the right upper lobe (series 6, image 60), with multiple adjacent micronodules up to 3 mm. No significant change is noted compared with the chest CT of 10/07/2025. Compared with the chest CT of 22/12/2023, the largest nodule has decreased in size, no cavitation is identified, and the number of micronodules has decreased. The previously described subsolid nodules (noted on the study from 12/22/2023) in the right lower lobe and left upper lobe are no longer visualized. Fleischner Society recommendations are not applicable.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Unremarkable.

Upper abdomen: Nonobstructing calculus (2 mm) in the upper pole of the right kidney.
val_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right upper lobe with multiple adjacent micronodules. No significant change is noted compared with the chest CT of 10/07/2025. Compared with the chest CT of 12/22/2023, the largest nodule has decreased in size, no cavitation is identified, and the number of micronodules has decreased. Given the history of suspected coccidioidomycosis on studies dated October 07, 2025 and December 22, 2023, and the presence of cavitary consolidation in the same region on the earlier study dated December 22, 2023, this lesion likely represents a residual post-infectious nodule.

2. Right renal calculus.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Lung nodule.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on 10/7/2025, 12/22/2023.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 2.25 mGy; total DLP 86 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple solid and nonsolid micronodules up to 2 mm and nodules up to 6 mm are noted in both lungs. Some of the solid nodules are cavitary. Areas of lung parenchyma with nodules in a "tree-in-bud" pattern are seen. These changes are unchanged compared with June 10, 2025. Mild bilateral reticular opacities are noted. Mild centrilobular emphysema is unchanged. Linear atelectatic changes are present in the right middle lobe and in the lingular and basal segments on the left (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical pleural thickening bilaterally (clinically insignificant). Mild thickening of the walls of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi is noted, and the lumens of a few segmental bronchi are filled with bronchial contents; the changes may correspond to bronchitis.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion measures up to 6 mm in thickness. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 136 HU, T12 - 113 HU, L1 - 115 HU, L2 - 111 HU. Ununited fractures of the right 6th and 7th ribs, healing fracture of the left 5th rib.

Upper abdomen: In the region of the posterior wall of the gastric fundus, an extraluminal rounded mass with lobulated well-defined contours is identified, showing interval growth, previously measuring 2.1 × 1.3 × 1.6 cm and currently measuring 2.0 × 2.6 × 2.2 cm. Increased number of visible visceral lymph nodes. A few para-aortic lymph nodes are enlarged up to 1.1 cm in short axis.
doc_impression:
1. Multiple bilateral nodules and micronodules; the changes may correspond to a mycobacterial process in the lungs.

2. Thickening of the walls of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi; the changes may correspond to bronchitis.

3. Mild centrilobular emphysema.

4. Calcification of the thoracic aorta.

5. Aortic valve calcification.

6. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

7. Minimal pericardial effusion.

8. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

9. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

10. Ununited displaced fractures of the right 6th and 7th ribs, healing fracture of the left 5th rib.

11. Gastric fundus mass, likely gastrointestinal stromal tumor. Further evaluation is needed.

12. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple solid and ground-glass nodules (predominantly peribronchial) in both lungs. Most of the solid nodules are cavitary.  Follow-up nodules:
-Right upper lobe: up to 8 mm (series 5, image 43) 
-Right lower lobe: up to 5 mm (series 5, image 127)
-Left lower lobe: up to 6 mm (series 5, image 119)
A solitary calcified nodule up to 3 mm in the right upper lobe. Nodules are unchanged compared with June 10, 2025. Mild centrilobular emphysema (unchanged). Linear atelectasis in the middle and lower lobes of the right lung.  Mild thickening of the walls of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi is noted, and the lumens of a few segmental bronchi are filled with bronchial contents; the changes may correspond to bronchitis (unchanged). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae, most pronounced in the L1 vertebral body, measuring up to +115 HU. Non-united fractures of the right 6th and 7th ribs.

Upper abdomen: In the posterior wall of the gastric fundus, an extraluminal mass measuring 2.0 × 2.6 × 2.2 cm is noted, previously measuring 2.1 × 1.3 × 1.6 cm.
val_impression:
1. Multiple bilateral nodules. Most likely represents a manifestation of infectious disease, such as mycobacterial infection or coccidioidomycosis. Stable findings compared with the chest CT of June 10, 2025.

2. Thickening of the walls of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi; the changes may correspond to bronchitis. Stable findings compared with the chest CT of June 10, 2025.

3. Gastric fundus mass, may be gastrointestinal stromal tumor. Increase compared with the previous study.

4. Mild centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

5. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

6. Calcification of the thoracic aorta. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

7. CT signs of bone demineralization, consistent with osteopenia as per densitometry data from 04/07/2025.

8. Non-united fractures of the right 6th and 7th ribs.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
